content.assessment: No comparable international case of compensatory comprehensive-school-quality investment (preserving tracking structure) is present in the supplied evidence. The cross-national findings instead concentrate on structural (tracking-age) reforms and their inequality effects, offering only indirect relevance: they suggest that while quality investment might help, cross-national evidence attributes much of Hungary's exceptional inequality to structural stratification (between-school variance) rather than resource quality alone.
opportunities: Could be evaluated against the OECD finding that within-school variance is not strongly tied to tracking age, supporting a rationale that quality-of-schooling investment operates on a different (potentially complementary) axis than structural timing
strengths: Consistent with cross-national finding that between-school variance, not within-school variance, drives tracking-related inequality; improving comprehensive-school quality could, in principle, reduce the between-school gap component
threats: Given Hungary's exceptionally high between-school variance (71% vs. OECD 36%), a strategy that leaves tracking structure intact risks being insufficient on the same axis that comparative evidence identifies as most distinctive and inequality-linked in Hungary
weaknesses: No directly comparable international mechanism (compensatory investment while preserving early tracking) is present in the evidence to validate transferability

Cross-national evidence indicates early tracking's effects operate largely through sorting composition (higher-status students entering selective tracks) rather than through resource differences alone, suggesting investment without altering tracking structure may not fully offset the compositional mechanism
